name: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B
description: Still widely deployed; baseline against RPi 5

release-date.comment: Launched 24 June 2019. The 8 GB SKU was released later, on 28 May 2020.

status.comment: Production guaranteed through January 2034 per Raspberry Pi's longevity statement; widely available through Approved Resellers as of April 2026.

architecture-family.comment: Cortex-A72 is Armv8-A 64-bit; both 32-bit (armhf) and 64-bit (arm64) Raspberry Pi OS images exist, but the silicon's primary ISA is arm64.

open-source-hardware.comment: Raspberry Pi publishes mechanical drawings and a partial schematic, but PCB design files are not released under an OSHW-compatible license.

country-of-origin.comment: Designed by Raspberry Pi (Cambridge, UK); manufactured at the Sony UK Technology Centre in Pencoed, Wales.

max-clock-speed-ghz.comment: Latest revisions clock at 1.8 GHz; earlier production runs shipped at 1.5 GHz.

process-node.comment: TSMC 28 nm per multiple secondary sources; Raspberry Pi has not officially published the exact node.

vulkan-support.comment: Vulkan 1.0 (later extended to 1.2 by the Mesa V3DV driver).

hw-video-decode.comment: H.265 (HEVC) decode at 4Kp60 and H.264 decode at 1080p60 in hardware. No VP9 or AV1 hardware decode.

hw-video-encode.comment: H.264 encode at 1080p30 in hardware. No HEVC, VP9, or AV1 hardware encode.

ram-min-gb.comment: 1 GB SKU was discontinued for a time and reintroduced; SKUs sold over the product lifetime are 1, 2, 4, and 8 GB.

emmc.comment: No onboard eMMC; storage is microSD plus optional USB/network boot.

m2-nvme-slot.comment: No onboard M.2 slot and no exposed PCIe lane; NVMe usage requires USB 3.0 enclosures or third-party adapters that piggyback on the SoC's USB controller.

wifi.comment: Dual-band 2.4/5 GHz 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) via Cypress CYW43455 combo chip.

usb-a-3x-ports.comment: 2× USB 3.0 ports (5 Gbps; the two ports share a single VL805 USB 3.0 controller's bandwidth).

usb-c-ports-data.comment: USB-C connector is power-only (5V DC, min 3A); does not carry USB data.

poe-support.comment: PoE/PoE+ via the official PoE/PoE+ HAT; not built into the board itself.

max-display-resolution.comment: Single display up to 4Kp60. Driving two displays simultaneously caps at 4Kp30 each.

displayport.comment: No DisplayPort output. USB-C is power-only with no DP-Alt mode.

audio-jack.comment: 4-pole 3.5 mm stereo audio + composite video jack.

rpi-compatible-header.comment: Standard Raspberry Pi 40-pin GPIO header (pin layout the rest of the ecosystem mirrors).

rpi-hat-compatible.comment: Reference HAT-compatible footprint with EEPROM auto-detect.

pcie-lanes-exposed.comment: No user-exposed PCIe; the BCM2711's PCIe lane is dedicated internally to the VL805 USB 3.0 controller.

power-input.comment: 5V DC via USB-C, minimum 3A; official 15W USB-C PSU recommended. GPIO 5V pins and PoE HAT are alternative paths.

idle-power-w.comment: ~2.7 W idle (540 mA @ 5 V) measured by Jeff Geerling's Pi Dramble project with no USB peripherals attached.

load-power-w.comment: ~6.4 W (1280 mA @ 5 V) at 400% CPU load (stress on all four cores), no USB peripherals.

cooling-required.comment: Operating range 0–50 °C ambient. Bare board will throttle under sustained load; a passive heatsink is sufficient for most workloads, with a fan only needed for sustained heavy loads or hot environments.

rtc-battery-header.comment: No onboard RTC and no battery header; users add an external RTC via I2C HAT or use NTP. (Pi 5 was the first flagship Pi with an onboard RTC.)

length-mm.comment: Official mechanical dimension 85.6 mm × 56.5 mm × 17 mm.

form-factor-class.comment: 85.6×56.5 mm — the canonical Pi-standard footprint that most other 'Pi-style' SBCs imitate.

weight-g.comment: ~46 g bare board; retailer listings vary 45–50 g across revisions.

mainline-linux-kernel.comment: Mainline support for the BCM2711 has matured since Linux 5.7 (June 2020); current 6.x kernels boot the Pi 4 with HDMI/KMS, USB, Ethernet, GPIO, and 3D acceleration working from the upstream defconfig. Vendor downstream kernel still preferred by Raspberry Pi OS for tighter firmware/codec integration, but mainline is functionally complete for this board.

official-os-image.comment: Raspberry Pi OS (Debian-based) is published and actively maintained — current images use kernel 6.12 on Debian 13 (Trixie).

distros-with-images.comment: Raspberry Pi OS (official), Ubuntu (Canonical-published), Debian (raspi.debian.net community images), Fedora (aarch64), Alpine, DietPi, Manjaro ARM all publish Pi 4 images. Armbian historically does not target the Raspberry Pi family.

bootloader.comment: Pi 4 uses Raspberry Pi's proprietary closed-source bootloader stored in onboard SPI EEPROM that loads Linux directly. U-Boot can run as a chainloaded second-stage but is not the primary bootloader.

long-term-support.comment: Production guaranteed through January 2034 — Raspberry Pi's stated longevity for the Pi 4.

android-support.comment: No official Android image. Community LineageOS/KonstaKANG and emteria builds exist but are unofficial.

msrp-base-usd.comment: Original launch MSRP $35 for the 1 GB SKU (June 2019). Snapshot 2026-04-26: street prices are elevated due to LPDDR4 supply constraints; the 1 GB SKU is no longer the typical entry point at retail.

msrp-top-usd.comment: 8 GB SKU launched at $75 (May 2020). Recent memory-driven hikes have pushed street prices for the 8 GB SKU well above $90 at major resellers (April 2026).

available-regions.comment: Sold globally via the Raspberry Pi Approved Reseller network.

typical-lead-time.comment: Snapshot 2026-04-26: in stock at major Approved Resellers (Pi Hut, Adafruit, CanaKit). Stock has fully normalized after the 2021–2023 shortage.

documentation-quality.comment: Comprehensive official datasheet, mechanical drawing, product brief, processor docs, and beginner-friendly Getting Started materials.

community-size.comment: Per RESEARCH.md threshold: 'huge' is reserved for Raspberry Pi only.

active-forum-discord.comment: Official Raspberry Pi Forums are extremely active and vendor-staffed.

accessory-ecosystem.comment: The Pi 4 has the second-largest SBC accessory ecosystem (after the Pi 5): official PoE+ HAT, Camera Modules, Touch Display, cases, fans, plus an extensive third-party HAT/case/screen market.
